title: Česká Třebová railway station

infobox.value: This article about a Czech railway station is a [stub](/wiki/Wikipedia:Stub "Wikipedia:Stub"). You can help Wikipedia by [adding missing information](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=%C4%8Cesk%C3%A1_T%C5%99ebov%C3%A1_railway_station&action=edit).

([Czech](/wiki/Czech_language "Czech language"): *Železniční stanice Česká Třebová*) is a major railway junction in the [Czech Republic](/wiki/Czech_Republic "Czech Republic"), located in the town of [Česká Třebová](/wiki/%C4%8Cesk%C3%A1_T%C5%99ebov%C3%A1 "Česká Třebová"). It was opened in 1845 on the railway between [Prague](/wiki/Prague "Prague") and [Olomouc](/wiki/Olomouc "Olomouc"), and four years later another line to [Brno](/wiki/Brno "Brno") was opened, making it one of the first railway junctions to be completed in the Czech lands, after [Břeclav](/wiki/B%C5%99eclav "Břeclav") and [Přerov](/wiki/P%C5%99erov "Přerov").[[1]](#cite_note-1)
Hosnedl, Jiří; Adamec, Vladimír (1977). *Československo A - Z. Průvodce na cesty* (in Czech). Prague: Olympia. pp. 59–60.
["Schéma linek dálkových vlaků ČD, 2016–17"](https://www.cd.cz/images/cdos/prilohy/mapa-dalkove-dopravy-11-12-2016.pdf) (PDF). *České dráhy*. Retrieved 2 November 2017.

**Česká Třebová railway station** ([Czech](/wiki/Czech_language "Czech language"): *Železniční stanice Česká Třebová*) is a major railway junction in the [Czech Republic](/wiki/Czech_Republic "Czech Republic"), located in the town of [Česká Třebová](/wiki/%C4%8Cesk%C3%A1_T%C5%99ebov%C3%A1 "Česká Třebová"). It was opened in 1845 on the railway between [Prague](/wiki/Prague "Prague") and [Olomouc](/wiki/Olomouc "Olomouc"), and four years later another line to [Brno](/wiki/Brno "Brno") was opened, making it one of the first railway junctions to be completed in the Czech lands, after [Břeclav](/wiki/B%C5%99eclav "Břeclav") and [Přerov](/wiki/P%C5%99erov "Přerov").[[1]](#cite_note-1)

Today it is served by international and intercity trains from Prague to Brno (and onwards to Austria, Slovakia and Hungary) and from Prague to Olomouc and [Ostrava](/wiki/Ostrava "Ostrava") (and onwards to Slovakia and Poland).[[2]](#cite_note-2)
